ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for lion's mane mushroom cheesy patty; closest parent is mushroom food product (FOODON_03315763).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC used as closest parent for processed mushroom-based patty; no exact code for lion's mane cheesy patty exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for lion's mane mushroom cheesy patty as a prepared food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One lion's mane mushroom cheesy patty weighing approximately 120 g, as typically served at a Singapore café or casual dining restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Mushroom, oyster, cooked (USDA SR Legacy 11998) adapted with cheese and breadcrumb coating components; composite estimate for breaded fried mushroom patty with cheese
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and USDA Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for lion's mane mushroom cheesy patty. Nutrient values estimated by component analysis: lion's mane mushroom (Hericium erinaceus) raw nutritional profile from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168436, Mushrooms, lion's mane, raw), combined with standard breaded coating (flour, egg, breadcrumbs) and processed cheese contribution. Energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate values reflect a composite of approximately 50% mushroom, 25% breaded coating, and 25% cheese by weight. Values are moderate-confidence estimates.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — breaded and fried vegetable/mushroom patty analogous estimate; reference food: battered/crumbed vegetables (GI ~50–55)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lion's mane mushroom cheesy patty. GI estimated at 52 based on analogous breaded and fried vegetable/mushroom patty foods from the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range 48–56 for similar battered/crumbed vegetable products. The dominant glycemic driver is the breadcrumb and flour coating. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 23.4) / 100 ≈ 12.2, using 120 g serving size with 19.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from cheese and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 120 g serving provides approximately 456 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-salt preparation, as regular consumption may contribute meaningfully to the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from breadcrumb coating
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 52 and a glycemic load of 12.2 per serving, this patty has a low-to-moderate glycemic impact; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese and frying medium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each serving contributes approximately 4.9 g saturated fat and 42 mg cholesterol per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider baked or air-fried preparation methods to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Lion's Mane Mushroom Cheesy Patty is a vegetarian savoury patty made from Hericium erinaceus (lion's mane mushroom), a distinctive edible fungus prized for its seafood-like texture and mild, slightly sweet flavour. The mushroom is coated in a seasoned breadcrumb and flour batter, pan-fried or deep-fried until golden and crispy on the outside, and filled or topped with melted processed cheese, yielding a contrast of crunchy exterior and tender, juicy interior. In Singapore, this dish is served as a main course or burger-style patty at casual dining establishments and café-style restaurants, reflecting the city-state's growing appetite for creative plant-forward alternatives to meat. Nutritionally, the patty provides moderate protein (approximately 8.5 g per 100 g) from both the mushroom and cheese components, along with carbohydrates from the breaded coating and fat from the cheese and frying oil. The sodium content is moderate-to-high due to cheese and seasoning, making portion awareness relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 52 (low category), driven primarily by the breadcrumb coating. Lion's mane mushroom is also noted in nutritional literature for its dietary fibre and beta-glucan content, which may support gut health and immune function. This dish is suitable for lacto-ovo vegetarians but is not vegan, gluten-free, or confirmed halal.
common_user_queries: Is lion's mane mushroom patty healthy?

Can I eat lion's mane mushroom patty if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central for lion's mane mushroom cheesy patty as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component analysis using USDA FoodData Central data for lion's mane mushroom (FDC ID 168436), standard breaded coating, and processed cheese. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar breaded vegetable/mushroom products. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite food in the source database; lion's mane mushroom has negligible vitamin D unless UV-exposed, and the value is not confirmed for this preparation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source for this composite estimate. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entry for lion's mane mushroom and is not estimated for this composite. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives' as this is a mushroom-based protein patty, not a grain or staple. The food_name has been normalised from the promotional menu name 'House-made Lion Mane Cheeeezy Patty' to the canonical English name 'Lion's Mane Mushroom Cheesy Patty'. Halal status is not confirmed as cheese and frying oil sourcing is unknown for this restaurant item. All nutrient values are moderate-confidence estimates based on component analysis and should be replaced with laboratory-measured values if available.
